summary: Gruber investigates evidence found in Mac OS X 10.5.3 that Apple is preparing to rebrand its .Mac online service. Localization strings in iCal, Safari, and Mail now use placeholders instead of hardcoding the .Mac name, suggesting a rename is imminent. He argues the current name is poor because it sounds silly and limits the service to Mac users when Apple clearly wants to extend syncing to iPhone and iPod users on any platform. Drawing on Apple's trademark filings from 2006 for 'Mobile Me,' he speculates this could be the new name, fitting Apple's strategic focus on mobility.
key_points: Hidden localization changes in OS X 10.5.3 reveal Apple is preparing to rename .Mac, with apps designed to pull the new name via Software Update
The .Mac name is problematic both aesthetically and strategically — it echoes Microsoft's .NET branding and excludes non-Mac users from the service's identity
Apple's new over-the-air push syncing for iPhone 2.0 is initially Exchange-only, creating an obvious opening for a revamped .Mac to serve non-Enterprise users
Apple filed four trademark applications for 'Mobile Me' in 2006, one describing services remarkably similar to what a modernized .Mac would offer
Apple's strategic focus under Jobs is mobility — iPhone, iPod, MacBook Air — making a mobile-oriented service name logical
money_quotes: You know it's a bad name when the gist of it is so similar to that of a Microsoft initiative like .NET.
Tying the service's name explicitly to 'Mac' would make it awkward to, say, introduce new features for over-the-network synching of data to iPhones and iPods belonging to Windows users.
As it stands now, these features will only be available for users of Microsoft Exchange. Awkward.
What is it that they're focused on? It's not the pro market. It's mobility — iPhone, iPod, MacBook Air.
It seems like it will be updated via Software Update once Apple renames .Mac and — boom! — all your apps will have the new name.
key_insight: Evidence buried in OS X 10.5.3 and old trademark filings suggests Apple is about to rebrand .Mac as 'Mobile Me,' reflecting its strategic pivot toward mobility and cross-platform syncing.
